And, you know, did the thing. That was a big round, too. Wasn't that a big round? Or, I mean, didn't they? It was a very big round. Us, Mayfield, and a few others. Yep. And, anyhow, brought in the big company guy. That didn't work out. And the advertising eyeball orientation in 2000 and 2001, 2002, you know, was completely changed. And so that was the early days of the internet. They were kind of one of the first preeminent financial advice sites. And 100% advertising, but 100%, you know, in those days, it was just kind of eyeballs. And so we had to create, they had to create a real business and business model. And they went about it starting in 2002 and, you know, five, six times a year, seven times a year, I would fly back to Washington, D.C. Helped them recruit a bunch of board members. We ended up pivoting to a subscription orientation. And today, very successful, cash flow positive, and, you know, kind of terrific company. Is there going to be an exit there? We exited. Oh, you exited. Okay. It was clear that the founders had a different exit orientation than we did. Yeah. And so the company bought us out. Yeah. If you could pick one activity that you talk about more than anything else to an entrepreneur, what would that be? Getting the team right. I just, you know, like real estate, people talk about location, location, location. I would say startups, to me, it's about people, people, people. And there's a funny story, getting back to Coach K. In 2000, after the world blew up in October of 2000, I had this incredible privilege to be with him for an hour and a half in his office at Cameron Indoor Stadium. And at one point, he looks over to me and he says, Dan, do you love your team? I said, wow, I hadn't really thought about that. And I think for certain people like him, loving your team is kind of a conditioned precedent requirement for being successful. And I hadn't really examined that for me. But 13 years later, it's pretty clear that in a bizarre way, he understood business far better than I did. And so I think what I try to talk to, if I, I mean, there's a lot of things, right? Because it's not, but if you have the right people that you rely on and trust and feel have a complimentary skill, then I think you have an advantage. And one of the things we ask ourselves, if we did a seed a year or two in, how is the person recruited? And if the, if we're two years in and we think this is a big idea and there's been no impact players hired, that kind of tells you something about either the space or the CEO. And I think there, the pattern that I've observed is the best, biggest companies always seem to be able to hire people they shouldn't hire because the CEO, the environment creates something that attracts people who've kind of been there, done that to balance out this incredible disruptive attitude. And it's that alchemy of, of experience and new that I think every company has to go through and transition. But if I had to pick one thing, it would be people. Yeah. People first, product second. But, you know, one of my other mentors is Bill Campbell and he's product, product, product, product. And what's, we've had a number of sessions where Howard spoke and Bill Campbell spoke. And Howard says, first thing you should hire is a, is a HR person. And Bill says, first thing you should hire is a product person. And so I think part of that is the, is a different orientation between classic consumer businesses and technology. But I think we're in this incredible time right now that we're all so privileged to live in where the intersection of consumer and technology is like it's never been before. And, you know, you see so many of these companies that three, four years ago they had a zero mobile penetration. And today it's 40%. So, I mean, we're living in a transformative thing. And my belief is that great consumer tech companies start from the inside out and you get the people right, then it flows to the customers. Yeah. And you figure it out, like you say, because you'll end up pivoting or something's going to change. If, if, what, what type of company would you like to meet? One that's creating some revenue and is growing or a company that with no revenue but with a billion dollar idea? No revenue led by a killer guy with a billion dollar idea. Because you're swinging for the fences. Because swinging for the fences doesn't, we're looking for. You can't hit a double. If you, if you, if you, yeah, yes. Okay, I'll stop sugarcoating. We're swinging for the fences. That's fair. That's fair. What makes Maveron different from other VC firms in the area? Or at Maxnot area, let's just say, what makes Maveron different than other VC firms in your opinion? Probably three or four things. First, the consumer only focus is just different. Yeah. It's a little unique. And frankly, I think we can easily partner with other VCs that have a different orientation. The world doesn't have to be a zero sum game. So first, consumer only. Second, our entrepreneurs tell us that they meet a broader spectrum of people from Maveron and our network than typically they meet from other firms. So we have a kind of team sport attitude that when we are at events, you know, it's not Dan's company. It's our company. And we try to figure out how we can each help entrepreneurs. And then lastly, kind of sounds corny. But it was told to me by an entrepreneur two weeks ago, they feel more family-like with us. We try to foster a no-assholes family orientation and collaborate. And we have a CEO forum for three days, two days every year. And I'm just humbled by the action or the attitude that people come out of it, that it's a different conference. Why? Because people let their guards down and talk about real problems as opposed to they tell me a lot of these conferences they go to. And there's a need to put up a facade. And we try to foster that by just being hungry but humble ourselves and realize that really it's the entrepreneur that is creating all the value in our business. And it's taken us a while to get total clarity on that, but that's true. Every great win we've had, it's a function of the entrepreneur and the team have created something because of their hard work and focus. Do I want to believe that at times we've added value? Absolutely. Are there situations where we've, you know, helped materially? I think so. Sure. But pushing all the credit to the people that create value I think is a good thing in life. And so we try to make it a little more family-esque. The world doesn't have to be a zero-sum game. Sure. I think in a certain sense there are so few great entrepreneurs with so few great ideas that it is somewhat of a zero-sum game amongst VCs. But once you're in the game and in the company, I think it doesn't have to be a zero-sum game. Yeah, you can still win. You guys are investing, you guys have been investing earlier and earlier. Like you used to not do any seed. Correct. And now you're like pretty early. Is that because of the trend that everyone reads about or why is that? Why are you investing earlier and earlier? Well, getting back to the challenges of 2009, we looked around at the VC landscape and we asked ourselves, where can we excel? And we had added a lot of people with operating experience and we saw that successful companies in B and C rounds just got very pricey. And we said it takes far less capital ever to document consumer acceptance and traction. So we could be putting less capital to work yet getting meaningful stakes. So getting back to trying to find our place in the ecosystem, we decided we couldn't compete with these later stage guys or, you know, certain firms that could raise a gazillion dollars and could put 50 million or 20 million to work in a series B. So we decided to move earlier and frankly create a pipeline of entrepreneurs, not just a pipeline of deals. Yeah. So again, going back to the people, investing in that entrepreneur. Yeah. At Mavron, everyone has, like you've said, everyone kind of has a specialty. You know, they have some special pieces to what they're really knowledgeable about. What's yours? What do you bring to the table? So, well, I think in any company that you've found, you have a certain role as... As founder. As founder and some responsibility in terms of leadership, unless it's clear that someone else is doing that. But I tend... There's a lot of different ways to be successful in VC. I think... And there's more ways to be mediocre than there are to be successful. I tend to be focused on the people side and that could be building a network of folks that source and help our companies. I tend to be kind of oriented toward trying to think through the psychological contract that we have with CEOs and their people issues. I'm not as strong a product orientation guy. Yeah. And, you know, I think the next chapter for me in my career, frankly, is helping some of the people in my firm, Rebecca, Jason, David, kind of watch them come up and be successful. And kind of transcend Maveron from something that Dan and Howard founded to an enduring institution that's recognized and respected as one of the leading funders of consumer businesses. Yeah. That's a good goal. What is one of your favorite stories about your first meeting of a founder? Is it the guy that met with Viggo Figuero? No. One guy cold called and said, he sent me an hourglass that was about this big. And he said. So he sent it and then he called. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. And he said, here's an hourglass. All I need is 18 minutes of your time or whatever. And it was kind of cool. So I took it. We flipped it over. And by the end of the 18 minutes, that was that. But it was kind of an interesting way to go. I think, you know, like it or not, if you look at our funnel, we get over a thousand plans a year. And we're going to make four to six core investments and, say, 15 to 20 seed investments. So if things aren't referred to us in some way or another, it's hard for us to really focus on it. Just because of the ratio of. Yeah. Volume. Yeah. Yeah. What has the. What company had the easiest exit? And why do you think that was? Are any exits easy? Well, not really. But you know what I mean. I mean, like, what's been the. What's been something that's like, wow, that was. That was a lot easier than I expected, maybe. Maybe that's a better way to phrase that. Easier than expected. I think our first investment was the easiest exit. eBay. You know, I mean. Because how long did you hold it? We invested on June 11th, 1998, and it went public at the end of September. So that was pretty tough. Well, they wanted Howard on the board, and so we were fortunate enough to participate. The company went public, then it went up, then the stock went down. Sure. And then over the holidays in 1998, I had gotten married in November. And over the holidays, my wife and I went to Asia for our honeymoon. And for whatever reason, stock kind of kept going up five to ten points every day. And it was the best honeymoon anyone had ever had. It was like, it was almost a fantasy. And. Making money every day. It was like, oh, this is. Are you kidding me? But, you know, eBay was both a blessing and a curse. Right? You start a business. First investment. Your first investment ends up being so successful. This is so easy. Exactly. Rose-colored glasses. You can say it's better to be lucky than smart, but unless you get really kicked in the you-know-whats, I don't think I'm capable of learning the lesson. So it was a mixed blessing. Yeah. But that was the easiest. Yeah. Yeah, you get, you actually, and in your career, you're thinking, wow, I'm pretty good at this. Right? Just starting out, I had a first investment. Well, our first annual meeting was in September of 99, and we had made four or five investments. One was eBay, and at the time it was like a 40 or 50X, and another was drugstore.com, and at the time it was a 10X. Wow. And people thought we were drinking something special. Audience, questions, feel free to go here, Chris, right in the middle. How do you work with angel groups, and give us a sense of your vision for the future VCOs companies? So one of our partners in San Francisco runs the, or ran the Internet Investment Committee at the Band of Angels. So he was an angel for three or four years. You know, we try to have angel groups understand where we're different, which is consumer only, and we look at three verticals, web-enabled consumer services, education, and wellness. And so we're kind of looking for outstanding people in those three areas, and try to at least let those groups know what we're doing. And hopefully find enough of a screen that they don't send us, you know, kind of everything that, so I think in any deal referral situation, I think it's the trust and confidence to know, okay, I've taken the time to understand what you want, and I'm not bringing you things that you don't want. In terms of the future of VC, we invested in a crowdfunding company called Circle Up down in San Francisco, and it's crowdfunding for early-stage consumer businesses. And it's fascinating because we don't invest in, you know, kind of food or beauty companies, but they have used the web to create this highly targeted, like AngelList, but more targeted group around consumer companies. And I think a lot of the consumer companies love it because they're attracting, or they're getting, in a very efficient way, exposure to a lot of people who can really add value as angels to them, who have pattern recognition about this stuff. So, I think, you know, you're going to see more and more of that. To me, there's been a lot of debate lately about how radical is the model going to change. My intuition is that the model for late-stage funding may change, in that once the platforms of influence and the relationships are created in the seed A and perhaps B round, then what does a company really need? In my mind, a lot of times it just needs more fuel to grow. But it doesn't need another person around the boardroom table. Sometimes it does, in which case it should seek that out. But in many times, particularly for successful companies, I think they're just looking for capital. And it feels to me like technology and the web can be a crossing function as, you know, these secondary markets and all these things have kind of come earlier on. But, you know, I mean, to me, in terms of early-stage stuff, I get back to it's all about the people. And you can have really good track records but not click with an entrepreneur. And, you know, we sold a business a few months ago. And we're at the closing dinner. And business was sold to a strategic. And, you know, the entrepreneur basically says to me, this was an okay outcome, but here's to our next one. And, you know, I mean, to me, that's what I live for is the people you want to back again being in a position to have them know you and you know them. So I think there will be some disruption and I think AngelList and certain things like that are going to work. But I caution people against looking at early-stage money as fungible. It's not. And early-stage money comes with an attitude. And make sure that the people on your bus are people you want on the bus. And, you know, when I've seen, you know, we were in a company here in town, Decide.com. Sure. Sold to eBay. Sure. Mike Fridgeton did a really nice job. And, you know, it was an incredible -- Mike interviewed the investors as much as we interviewed him. Yeah. And, you know, we had Madrona in the A. We did the B. Vulcan did the C. And it was a wonderful experience because we were respectful of the entrepreneur and of each other. And it was kind of a complete collaboration. And I think Mike fostered that. You know, I remember the day we're signing the term sheet, he says, "Can I come over to your office? I'd like to sign the term sheet with you in person." That's interesting. So he comes over. And, you know, when he signs the term sheet, he says, "What would this be to really be one of your best investments? Because that's where I'd like to start." Right. And it was just a great psychological grounding of how much he cared. And that followed through to the boardroom dynamics. And it was just a much more pleasant experience. Yeah. Yeah. What keeps you going, Dan, and what motivates you, you know, when you fail to get back up and keep doing what you need? And how do you create -- how do you help Maveron become an enduring business? And how do you lift up the people who have trusted you and wanted to be part of our team and have them inherit that mantle? So I get back to what I love about Duke basketball is it's bigger than any individual. And I'm trying to help Maveron become bigger than Howard or I. And, you know, give back to the people who trusted us and watch them grow. And I couldn't have said that, frankly, in my 30s or 40s because I hadn't achieved a level of grounding and satisfaction amongst myself. And so I feel fortunate that I'm able to try and do that. All the way in the back, very back row. Sorry for such serious topics. People say I'm serious. You know, let me -- you know, in our business, if your takeaway is that we're batting 600 or 700, then I've created the wrong takeaway. You know, this is hard. But the internal workings are you pick a strategy and you ignore the data. And you're really slow to pivot. There's too much, even in today's reduced environment, there's still too much capital chasing too few great entrepreneurs attacking big ideas. segments.text: Welcome to the Star of Grime.
Before we get going, can I just have a Sofia Vergara story?
I don't have one.
I have one.
Okay, yeah.
Because you just mentioned her.
So, I was at a friend's 60th birthday party in Greece in July.
And we're out, has anyone been to Mykonos?
Okay, so you know how crazy Mykonos is.
So, it's about one or two in the morning, and we're at this nightclub with a group of people.
And my wife comes over to me and says, do you know who Sofia Vergara is?
And of course you don't.
I don't know who Sofia Vergara is.
And she explains to me that there was a guy who was there at the nightclub who she was talking to.
Because I saw her talking to this guy.
And they were talking for about seven or eight minutes, and then she comes over to me.
Well, as it turns out, it's a guy who has a company, and he had sent Mavron a business plan.
And so, here we are.
It's 2 a.m. in Mykonos, in the connected world we live in.
And he says to her, I think that's Dan Levitan over there.
And she's like, I've never had my husband be so popular.
What's going on here?
And she figures if she gets friendly with this guy, then she'll get the intro to Sofia Vergara.
So, if you go to my wife's Facebook page, Stacy Levitan, you'll see a big picture of her with Sofia Vergara.
And the quid pro quo was, she introduced me to this entrepreneur, who's a guy named Nick Loeb, who was actually highlighted in the New York Times over the weekend for a business called Onion Crunch.
And anyhow, that's my Sofia Vergara story.
It fell flat a little, but I thought it was a good idea.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Exactly.
Well, we normally don't get to, you know, we say those things, but we don't actually have stories about the people.
So, Dan, again, welcome.
Thanks for coming.
I know you've got a busy schedule.
I know it's a busy week for you.

Getting the team right.
I just, you know, like real estate, people talk about location, location, location.
I would say startups, to me, it's about people, people, people.
And there's a funny story, getting back to Coach K.
In 2000, after the world blew up in October of 2000, I had this incredible privilege to be with him for an hour and a half in his office at Cameron Indoor Stadium.
And at one point, he looks over to me and he says, Dan, do you love your team?
I said, wow, I hadn't really thought about that.
And I think for certain people like him, loving your team is kind of a conditioned precedent requirement for being successful.
And I hadn't really examined that for me.
But 13 years later, it's pretty clear that in a bizarre way, he understood business far better than I did.
And so I think what I try to talk to, if I, I mean, there's a lot of things, right?
Because it's not, but if you have the right people that you rely on and trust and feel have a complimentary skill, then I think you have an advantage.
And one of the things we ask ourselves, if we did a seed a year or two in, how is the person recruited?
And if the, if we're two years in and we think this is a big idea and there's been no impact players hired, that kind of tells you something about either the space or the CEO.

And I think there, the pattern that I've observed is the best, biggest companies always seem to be able to hire people they shouldn't hire because the CEO, the environment creates something that attracts people who've kind of been there, done that to balance out this incredible disruptive attitude.
And it's that alchemy of, of experience and new that I think every company has to go through and transition.
But if I had to pick one thing, it would be people.
Yeah.
People first, product second.
But, you know, one of my other mentors is Bill Campbell and he's product, product, product, product.
And what's, we've had a number of sessions where Howard spoke and Bill Campbell spoke.
And Howard says, first thing you should hire is a, is a HR person.
And Bill says, first thing you should hire is a product person.
And so I think part of that is the, is a different orientation between classic consumer businesses and technology.
But I think we're in this incredible time right now that we're all so privileged to live in where the intersection of consumer and technology is like it's never been before.
And, you know, you see so many of these companies that three, four years ago they had a zero mobile penetration.
And today it's 40%.
So, I mean, we're living in a transformative thing.
And my belief is that great consumer tech companies start from the inside out and you get the people right, then it flows to the customers.

What makes Maveron different from other VC firms in the area?
Or at Maxnot area, let's just say, what makes Maveron different than other VC firms in your opinion?
Probably three or four things.
First, the consumer only focus is just different.
Yeah.
It's a little unique.
And frankly, I think we can easily partner with other VCs that have a different orientation.
The world doesn't have to be a zero sum game.
So first, consumer only.
Second, our entrepreneurs tell us that they meet a broader spectrum of people from Maveron and our network than typically they meet from other firms.
So we have a kind of team sport attitude that when we are at events, you know, it's not Dan's company.
It's our company.
And we try to figure out how we can each help entrepreneurs.
And then lastly, kind of sounds corny.
But it was told to me by an entrepreneur two weeks ago, they feel more family-like with us.
We try to foster a no-assholes family orientation and collaborate.
And we have a CEO forum for three days, two days every year.
And I'm just humbled by the action or the attitude that people come out of it, that it's a different conference.
Why?
Because people let their guards down and talk about real problems as opposed to they tell me a lot of these conferences they go to.
And there's a need to put up a facade.
And we try to foster that by just being hungry but humble ourselves and realize that really it's the entrepreneur that is creating all the value in our business.
And it's taken us a while to get total clarity on that, but that's true.
Every great win we've had, it's a function of the entrepreneur and the team have created something because of their hard work and focus.
Do I want to believe that at times we've added value?
Absolutely.
Are there situations where we've, you know, helped materially?
I think so.
Sure.
But pushing all the credit to the people that create value I think is a good thing in life.
And so we try to make it a little more family-esque.
The world doesn't have to be a zero-sum game.
Sure.
I think in a certain sense there are so few great entrepreneurs with so few great ideas that it is somewhat of a zero-sum game amongst VCs.
But once you're in the game and in the company, I think it doesn't have to be a zero-sum game.
Yeah, you can still win.

Well, getting back to the challenges of 2009, we looked around at the VC landscape and we asked ourselves, where can we excel?
And we had added a lot of people with operating experience and we saw that successful companies in B and C rounds just got very pricey.
And we said it takes far less capital ever to document consumer acceptance and traction.
So we could be putting less capital to work yet getting meaningful stakes.
So getting back to trying to find our place in the ecosystem, we decided we couldn't compete with these later stage guys
or, you know, certain firms that could raise a gazillion dollars and could put 50 million or 20 million to work in a series B.
So we decided to move earlier and frankly create a pipeline of entrepreneurs, not just a pipeline of deals.
Yeah.

Yeah.

How do you work with angel groups, and give us a sense of your vision for the future VCOs companies?
So one of our partners in San Francisco runs the, or ran the Internet Investment Committee at the Band of Angels.
So he was an angel for three or four years.
You know, we try to have angel groups understand where we're different, which is consumer only, and we look at three verticals, web-enabled consumer services, education, and wellness.
And so we're kind of looking for outstanding people in those three areas, and try to at least let those groups know what we're doing.

And hopefully find enough of a screen that they don't send us, you know, kind of everything that, so I think in any deal referral situation, I think it's the trust and confidence to know, okay, I've taken the time to understand what you want, and I'm not bringing you things that you don't want.
In terms of the future of VC, we invested in a crowdfunding company called Circle Up down in San Francisco, and it's crowdfunding for early-stage consumer businesses.

And it's fascinating because we don't invest in, you know, kind of food or beauty companies, but they have used the web to create this highly targeted, like AngelList, but more targeted group around consumer companies.

And I think a lot of the consumer companies love it because they're attracting, or they're getting, in a very efficient way, exposure to a lot of people who can really add value as angels to them, who have pattern recognition about this stuff.
So, I think, you know, you're going to see more and more of that.
To me, there's been a lot of debate lately about how radical is the model going to change.

My intuition is that the model for late-stage funding may change, in that once the platforms of influence and the relationships are created in the seed A and perhaps B round, then what does a company really need?
In my mind, a lot of times it just needs more fuel to grow.
But it doesn't need another person around the boardroom table.
Sometimes it does, in which case it should seek that out.
But in many times, particularly for successful companies, I think they're just looking for capital.
And it feels to me like technology and the web can be a crossing function as, you know, these secondary markets and all these things have kind of come earlier on.
But, you know, I mean, to me, in terms of early-stage stuff, I get back to it's all about the people.
And you can have really good track records but not click with an entrepreneur.
And, you know, we sold a business a few months ago.
And we're at the closing dinner.
And business was sold to a strategic.
And, you know, the entrepreneur basically says to me, this was an okay outcome, but here's to our next one.
And, you know, I mean, to me, that's what I live for is the people you want to back again being in a position to have them know you and you know them.
So I think there will be some disruption and I think AngelList and certain things like that are going to work.
But I caution people against looking at early-stage money as fungible.
It's not.
And early-stage money comes with an attitude.
And make sure that the people on your bus are people you want on the bus.

And how do you create -- how do you help Maveron become an enduring business?
And how do you lift up the people who have trusted you and wanted to be part of our team and have them inherit that mantle?

And I'm trying to help Maveron become bigger than Howard or I.
And, you know, give back to the people who trusted us and watch them grow.

And so I feel fortunate that I'm able to try and do that.
All the way in the back, very back row.
Sorry for such serious topics.
People say I'm serious.
You know, let me -- you know, in our business, if your takeaway is that we're batting 600 or 700, then I've created the wrong takeaway.
You know, this is hard.
But the internal workings are you pick a strategy and you ignore the data.
And you're really slow to pivot.
And, you know, everyone's kind of still living on the investment thesis that they invested behind.
And it's a year or two in.
And, you know, we're going to make this work.
We're going to work harder.
We're going to, you know, broaden our product.
And I think, you know, most of the time when it's a challenge, it's hard to pivot.
And it's emotionally hard.
And both the people around the table and the CEO sometimes can do it and sometimes they can't.
But I think the biggest dynamic is how do you, as a group, help look at data and ask what's too impulsive and therefore you shouldn't move that fast or, you know, how you should pivot.
And I would say ignoring the data in certain circumstances is the worst sin.
And I think it happens because people are too committed to the initial hypothesis.
And I would also say in a lot of boardrooms, there's the 800-pound gorilla that's under the table that no one has the guts to talk about.
And I get back to it's the CEO's job in my mind to orchestrate a group of people who are transparent and can work together so that there is no 800-pound gorilla.
You can talk about these things.
And I found that I have some CEOs who are -- I can make the decision myself.
And here's what I think we should do.
And then there's others that need to iterate and create a discussion and get input.
You know, some of us are kind of not burdened by self-doubt and are intuitive.
And others of us like to analyze.
And so I think knowing who you are and creating that environment around the table that makes you be successful as the CEO in your company is really important.

So the question is, for early stage investments, young entrepreneurs, how do you handle those guys that are early on in their careers, maybe the first time?
Yeah.

I think we do handle them slightly different in the sense that, you know, I mean, the reality of our business is if we put $100,000 into a seed, we probably need to treat it differently than if we put $5 million into an A.
That said, what we say to the entrepreneur is, in doing the seed, we want to earn the right to do your A.
And so that means that we need enough connectivity and enough help that they feel we've earned the right to do the A.
And in a number of circumstances here in town and other places, we've succeeded in that.
And occasionally we haven't looked at the situation as objectively as a new investor.
And that's painful.

But, you know, I think ultimately creating a cadence of reporting and understanding enough to be helpful and then creating the right opening with the CEO when he or she needs high impact decision making that we can help them think through it is what we try to do.
No, I think it's easier in certain ways to start a startup in Seattle.
This Valley Seattle thing, I think, is nonsense.
I think a lot of Rich Barton's thoughts on this make a lot of sense to me, which is this is a wonderful, wonderful place to start a company.
I mean, I look at Zulily as an example, plenty of technology talent, plenty of consumer talent.
And these guys flew below the radar screen for a long period of time, hired an awesome team.
That same company in the Valley would have been scrutinized a lot more.
And, you know, I've lived this, you know, they started in our office in November of 2009.
And really until Andreessen Horowitz invested in them in 2012.
No one knew about them.
Very few people knew about them.
And so they picked up ahead of steam.
And so I love Seattle as a place to build a company.
I think Seattle would benefit from greater, substantive, bold thinking.
And when Barton talks about, you know, Seattle entrepreneurs need to be more audacious, I think it's a hard thing to talk about.
But if you're a VC and you're looking for these kind of multi-hundred-million-dollar, billion-dollar companies, you need people who believe they can create those.
And who are capable of finding ideas in those spaces that they can create them.
But I think in terms of the environment of hiring people, clearly it's easier here.
The loyalty of employees, I think clearly it's easier here.
And I think the lack of scrutiny, Pando Daily and Crunchbase talking about the five startups that are doing the same thing as you increases your likelihood of success.
So I think this is a fantastic place to start a company.
And this nonsense about there's no money, BS.
The world doesn't need N+1 VCs.
The shortage is great entrepreneurs.
There's too much, even in today's reduced environment, there's still too much capital chasing too few great entrepreneurs attacking big ideas.
So when we started in 1998, we asked ourselves why should we exist?
And Howard and I decided that we had a different view that technology was integrating into consumers' lives.
And we wanted to create an expertise that focused on that.
So that's the marketing pitch on why we do that.
The other marketing pitch is if you create a lot of B2C companies, they can help each other.
But the reality is we dabbled in enterprise and we sucked at it.
And getting back to humility, this is a humbling business.
It's really hard to be good.
On average, people don't make money as VCs other than fees.
And that's not how we want to make money.
So we asked ourselves, and I think every startup should ask themselves this, what do you do better than other people?
And how does that concentration work in your favor?
So basically a pragmatic reality of trying to say, what do we do well?
And I will say in the enterprise-oriented deals that I've done, I haven't been able to help the entrepreneur as much as I'd like to.
And my team, same way.
